questions.prompt: Put these commands in the order you'd run them to identify a customer's registrar and DNS host from scratch.
items: WHOIS / RDAP lookup to identify the registrar
Confirm the nameserver hostnames belong to the expected DNS host (Cloudflare, Microsoft, GoDaddy, etc.)
Cross-check by querying the SOA record's MNAME field
questions.explanation: Identifying registrar and host is the first 30 seconds on any unfamiliar customer's DNS. Get this wrong and every subsequent edit goes to the wrong panel.

questions.prompt: Which records in a Cloudflare zone should be DNS-only (grey cloud) rather than proxied? (Select all that apply.)
choices: An A record pointing at the customer's mail server
An A record pointing at the customer's website origin
An autodiscover CNAME for Microsoft 365
A CNAME for an SaaS verification flow that resolves the hostname
A CNAME for the customer's marketing site
questions.explanation: Mail (SMTP/IMAP) doesn't proxy through HTTPS edge. Autodiscover and SaaS verification flows expect specific endpoint behaviour that the proxy interferes with. The web origin and marketing CNAME are exactly what proxy mode is for.

questions.prompt: A customer can have multiple v=spf1 TXT records at the apex, one for each mail-sending service.
questions.explanation: Only one v=spf1 record is allowed per domain. Multiple records cause SPF to fail entirely. The right pattern is one record with multiple include: directives, one per service.

questions.prompt: Put these DMARC rollout steps in the right order.
items: Audit every sender that legitimately sends mail as the customer's domain
Build a single SPF record covering all of them; enable DKIM at every sender
Publish DMARC at p=none with rua= reporting
Read aggregate reports for ~2 weeks; fix any legitimate sender failing alignment
Move to p=quarantine, watch reports, then move to p=reject
questions.explanation: Going straight to p=reject before SPF / DKIM alignment is verified breaks legitimate mail. The reporting stage is what makes the rollout safe.

questions.prompt: Cloudflare's CNAME flattening at the apex returns valid A and AAAA records to resolvers, even though the customer added what looks like a CNAME. What's the operational tradeoff?
choices: Cloudflare resolves the CNAME target periodically and caches the IPs; if the target's IPs change before the cache expires, brief outages occur
Flattening only works if DNSSEC is enabled
Flattening prevents DKIM CNAMEs from working
Flattening forces all records on the zone to be proxied
questions.explanation: Flattening replaces the CNAME at query time with the resolved IPs. Cloudflare caches the resolution; if the target's IPs change and Cloudflare's cache hasn't refreshed, the customer's apex resolves to stale IPs until the next refresh.

questions.prompt: Outlook is failing to auto-configure for a new user, asking for the server name. Which DNS record is most likely missing or wrong?
choices: The MX at the apex
The autodiscover CNAME or SRV record
The DMARC TXT at _dmarc
The SPF TXT at the apex
questions.explanation: Outlook walks autodiscover.<domain>/autodiscover/autodiscover.xml first, then falls back to the _autodiscover._tcp SRV record. If neither resolves, the user has to enter the server manually. MX, DMARC, and SPF affect mail flow / authentication, not auto-config.

questions.prompt: An SRV record has format _service._protocol.<domain> ... priority weight port target. Why is this more complex than an MX record?
choices: Because MX records are deprecated
Because SRV needs to communicate not just which host, but which port to connect on, and how to balance among multiple records
Because SRV records work for any TLD but MX records only work for .com
Because SRV records require DNSSEC
questions.explanation: SRV is generic service discovery: it carries host, port, priority, and weight in one record. MX is specifically for mail and assumes port 25 (or the receiving server's accepted SMTP port). SRV's generality is what makes it useful for autodiscover, SIP, and other service flows.

questions.prompt: Microsoft 365 uses two DKIM selector CNAMEs (selector1 and selector2) so it can rotate one signing key while the other stays live.
questions.explanation: M365 publishes two selectors and rotates between them. The CNAMEs point at hostnames Microsoft owns; Microsoft updates the actual public-key TXT records there during rotation, invisibly to the customer.

questions.prompt: A vendor wizard says 'add a TXT record at _vendorname.<your-domain>'. The technician adds it at the apex (@) instead. What happens?
choices: The verification works because all TXT records on the zone are checked
The verification fails because the wizard queries the specific name and only finds nothing there
The verification times out because the apex doesn't allow TXT records
The verification works after a 24-hour propagation delay
questions.explanation: Verification queries query a specific name. If the wizard expects the record at _vendorname.<domain> and you put it at the apex, the wizard sees nothing and rejects the verification. Adding records at the wrong name is one of the most common helpdesk mistakes.

questions.prompt: A customer is on Cloudflare for DNS hosting. Where do they renew their domain lease?
choices: At Cloudflare, because they manage DNS
At the original registrar, regardless of who hosts DNS
Renewal happens automatically when DNS is hosted on Cloudflare
At ICANN directly
questions.explanation: Renewal is a registrar function, separate from DNS hosting. Moving DNS to Cloudflare doesn't move the lease. The registrar still bills and renews; only Cloudflare Registrar (a separate product) actually moves the lease too.

questions.prompt: A customer's SPF record passes its check but starts failing intermittently. The record hasn't changed. What's a likely cause?
choices: DKIM was enabled and is now in conflict
The included senders' own SPF records grew, pushing total DNS lookups past SPF's 10-lookup limit
The DMARC policy was upgraded to p=reject
Cloudflare proxy was enabled on the apex
questions.explanation: SPF allows at most 10 DNS lookups during evaluation, including the lookups inside every include: reference. If a vendor adds new mechanisms to their SPF, the customer's record may quietly cross the limit and start failing for messages that were previously fine.

questions.prompt: Putting an A record for a mail server behind Cloudflare's orange-cloud proxy makes the mail server faster.
questions.explanation: Cloudflare's proxy is HTTPS-only. Mail traffic (SMTP, IMAP, POP) doesn't proxy through it; behind orange cloud, mail simply stops working. Mail-server records must be DNS-only (grey cloud).
